name: mandrake
mechanism: Mandragora species contain tropane alkaloids (for example hyoscyamine/scopolamine-like compounds) that exert antimuscarinic effects; available mechanistic data are preclinical and do not establish therapeutic efficacy.
effects: Anticholinergic toxicity patterns are documented in clinical toxicology literature.
Preclinical chemistry work supports tropane-alkaloid biosynthesis variability across Mandragora species.
contraindications: Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding because safety has not been established and anticholinergic toxicity is plausible.
Contraindicated in people at risk for anticholinergic complications (for example narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention risk).
safetyNotes: Risk of delirium, tachycardia, dry mouth, urinary retention, and confusion from anticholinergic exposure.
Toxicity management requires medical supervision; non-clinical ingestion is high risk.
description: Mandrake is a Solanaceae plant historically documented in Mediterranean and Middle Eastern materia medica and ritual contexts. Modern publications focus on tropane alkaloid chemistry and poisoning management concerns.
dosage: No safe self-care dosage is established in reviewed sources; toxicology-focused literature should guide cautionary copy only.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. interactions: Potential additive anticholinergic burden with other anticholinergic medicines.
Potential additive CNS effects with sedatives or alcohol; toxicity risk may increase.
preparation: Traditional preparations include roots in decoctions or topical folkloric preparations, but modern safety literature does not support unsupervised internal use.
region: Mediterranean basin and parts of Western/Central Asia in traditional records.
traditionalUse: Traditional records describe sedative, ritual, and analgesic uses; these historical uses should not be interpreted as evidence of modern clinical efficacy.
summary: Mandrake (Mandragora spp.) contains tropane alkaloids with anticholinergic activity; modern literature emphasizes toxicity risk and the need for strict safety framing rather than self-medication claims.
